sentences.text: Oh, I remember there were certain times when I was forbidden to go out of the house, or to go play with someone.
The-- periodically, German-- the motorcycle with the sidecar and a truck full of Germans would drive through.
They-- then the children were being-- would be kept inside.
And there was fear, and we could feel it.
And we never knew from day to day what was happening.
I-- I think people's tempers were short.
I-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there were no goodies.
I-- I know I didn't remember what any-- what any certain foods looked like.
It was very, very-- there was hunger.
The-- we-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I just remember that the family stayed a lot together as much as they could, in whatever manner, in-- in the house.
We-- As I said, my grandfather's business was the leather, the shoes.
And they were forced to make the shoes for the Germans.
So every now and then, they would come to the house.
And I'd-- all I remember is that there's-- even to this day, there seemed to be such an-- such an arrogance in-- in the stance of the-- of the-- of the men, the German men that came.
As if no one counted, and-- flies on a wall.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I guess I was one of the lucky ones, because I didn't live in the ghetto very long.
Well, we all had to have the star on our clothes.
And there wasn't much clothing-- whatever we had.
There was no way to purchase anything.
There was no way to make anything, whether it was material.
Whatever we had, we made do.
As children grew, we-- we gave to-- to the littler ones.
It's-- there's no-- winters were hard, because as the clothes became worn, there was-- there was no new clothes.
I remember that when I went to the farm, the-- I grew out of the clothes that I had brought with me.
And she made me-- my-- my parents had taken bedding into the forest, and they turned all this over to the people that I was living with.
So she emptied one of the big pillows and made me a dress out of the-- the pillow cover, which was not-- was not the kind of pillow cases that we have here.
It was like a-- almost like a canvas material.
I had no shoes.
I ran around barefoot in the winter time.
The closest thing I had to a shoe was like a Dutch clog with a wooden sole, and a little bit of leather on top.
So that's about it for clothes.
The first new dress I had was when my uncle took me to Kiev and got me a couple of dresses.
I don't recall any to speak of, but there were illnesses due to malnutrition, due to-- there were-- I don't ever remember seeing a doctor in the ghetto.
If there was one, I-- I don't know.
If I got ill, or-- my mother-- you know, the mother takes care of the child.
I-- I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the colds, the things like that-- but I do not recall ever seeing a doctor in the ghetto.
No, because when he-- when my Uncle Chaim and my grandfather left the ghetto-- later my grandfather wasn't able to go, only my Uncle Chaim.
But they went directly from the ghetto under escort to the German offices, or whatever.
I had never seen them.
Left the goods that they made for them, and were escorted back.
Later on in the ghetto, when my Uncle Chaim was allowed-- when he started going by himself, he was allowed to go on his own.

And this is how he-- he would take different routes, because they felt that with the whole family in the ghetto, that they-- and him-- that they could-- in a way, I guess they became so-- so convinced that he wouldn't do anything, that they came to trust him after so many trips back and forth.
And that's when he was able to-- to contact-- speaking to the younger men in the ghetto, those that were able to-- to leave for a while-- one of the men that-- he said he needed a helper.
And the helper never came back to the ghetto.
And this was one of the boys that also became a partisan.
I don't know what happened to him.
But-- The area there was-- was not-- it was a small town type area.
It was not a big city, so for us, the news was very scarce.
And we-- many ways, I felt isolated.
Whatever stories that he would come back with were not privy to my young ears, and I don't remember too much of that.
You know, when things were quiet, we played in the streets.
Also, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there was a lady who took several of the children and would try to teach us to-- the alphabet, little songs, things like that.
There was no school to speak of, but they-- they tried in many ways, each little group, to do something for the children.
I don't remember any of the stories, but I know that I had begun to learn to read and write.
And I had proof of that a little while ago.
I have-- I have relatives in Toronto, Canada that I went to visit.
And my uncle by marriage there showed me a letter that I had written, a few words, and my father had written to him.
This part of the family got sent to Siberia by the Russians, and my father wrote them a letter.
So that-- on the back, I wrote that I was upset with my cousin, Julie, because she had-- she didn't write back to me.
And of course, Julie, being two years younger than I, couldn't write back to me.
But they did do that-- they tried to keep a semblance of normalcy.
Yes, I heard about the beatings.
They tried to keep the children away.
We were very-- stay in the house.
But you would hear when the-- when the grown ups would talk.
This one got beaten, this one's beard got cut off, this one's peyots got cut off, this one got taken, and nobody knows where.
These things happened quite often.
I was witness only once to-- where several people were taken and put on a truck.
I don't know why.
We-- several of us children were just walking around, and we happened to come up to this house where they were taking them out.
And it was one woman and two men, and they were put on the truck and taken away.
But as small as the ghetto was, the children didn't know everybody.
So I don't know who they were.

In leaving for the forest, my Uncle Chaim took us out at night through a part that they had-- they had cut and camouflaged the wires, because the ghetto was-- was surrounded by barbed wire, with one gate in and out.
And they-- the-- the area, as I said, had many forests.
I remember the forests more clearly than anything else.

And through a partition there that they had made-- that he had made over the weeks or so that he had been accomplishing this-- we-- my mother and father and my grandfather and I were the-- he took us out first.
And he took us to an area in the forest that he had made ready.
And I remember the leaves were gold, and the trees were so tall.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Then about a week later, he brought his younger sister, my Aunt Chava, and my Uncle Sana into the forest.
There was another family there whom I don't remember at all.
But this little group lived there.
We-- strange, I don't remember what we ate.
I-- I don't remember any of the food, but we must have had something.
I don't know where it came from.
The Germans were becoming more and more active.
The patrols through the areas were almost on a nightly basis, and we would go into our underground shelter.
And when my Uncle Chaim, who was always on the move-- they had a signal that it was him when he came during the day, so that we-- we opened this little square opening.
And there were times when we came out in the daylight when it was quiet.
And of course, the few men that were there were posted as sentries, so that if there was any movement, either by Germans Ukrainians-- because the Ukrainians were very anti-Semitic.
They would betray you.
And it was just as bad to be caught by one of them as by a German patrol.
You mean between the grown-ups?
Yes, there were discussions.
Discussions about what was going on, or what they could do to survive.
The saving of the children was probably the most talked about topic.
How could we save the children.
And this is why so many of us were placed in Gentile homes.
Because some way, somebody had to be left.
I know that my parents spoke to me, don't-- this is your name from now on.
You don't have any parents from now on.
You are a cousin to these people, and you don't know anything.
If somebody speaks to you in Yiddish, you don't understand.
From this point on, you are a little Ukrainian girl.
My hair was-- I had long hair, long braids, and they cut my hair.
So don't ask me why, but I was supposed to look less Jewish.
You become attuned to the-- to the atmosphere of dos and don'ts.
Don't sing now, don't talk now, don't go outside now, don't-- a lot of don'ts.
Above all, try and be almost invisible when there are Germans around, or when there are people that you don't know around.
And you learn it.
You learn it fast.
I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think that in some way, it's left a mark on me of-- don't-- don't talk too loud-- don't-- insecurity, unsureness, maybe sometimes timidness.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Because even-- [SIGHS] even when the-- when the Russians liberated that part of Ukraine, the atmosphere for the Jews was not good.
And when nobody knows you're Jewish, you hear a lot.
And Jude this, and Jude that.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And my Uncle Sana, who was a hero and decorated and rescued, and went with the partisans all the way into the Carpathians, asked for permission to go to Poland.
He was denied.
And we had to walk from Kiev to Lublin, hitchhiking on locomotives.
Because when my uncle went to Lublin to see if there were any survivors, he found a sister, a brother-in-law, and two daughters-- two of their daughters, two little girls.
And he wanted permission to go back to live in Poland, because that's all the family that we thought we had left.
And from that point on, after hiding from the Germans and fighting with the Russians, he was in hiding from the Russians.
And he worked his way to Cyprus and to Israel before the-- before the Independence War, and got there in time to fight for independence in Israel.
When I lived with him in Kiev, after the liberation, I did go to school-- to Russian school, and Ukrainian school.
And
Yes.
My-- my mother did not come with us.
I said goodbye to her in the forest.
My Uncle Chaim and my father took me to the farmhouse.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] She told me to be a good girl and to listen to the lady that was going to take care of me, and that she would try and see me as much as she could.
My father was in the farmhouse in hiding the rest of the day.
And that night, he left to go back to my mother.
And he did the same, more or less-- hugged me and told me to remember all the things they had told me, and that he would try to see me when he could.
He just said to be good.
Yes, I can.
They come back to me a lot.
It's a feeling of tremendous aloneness.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] You feel like you're out by yourself, all alone.
You-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]
You don't-- you learn not to trust anyone.
It comes to where can I even trust my mother?
She said she'd come and see me, and she hasn't come.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] It's a lot of alone.
I-- I don't know how else to tell you this.
Do you have any recollection of what you might have said to your parents at that moment, or wanted to say?
I said I didn't want them to leave me, that I want to go with them.
And they said that I had to do what they said.
That it was important for me to obey them.
And that they would come and see me, and not to worry, and to do what I was told.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And I did.
She was kind.
There wasn't-- she took me into the house, and she sat me down and talked to me for a little while, and told me that she would be with me a lot.
And I would help her watch the cows and feed the chickens and the geese.
And that someday, my mother and father would come, and I would go back with them.
But until then, if anybody asked me who are you, that I was her cousin and I had come to live with them because my house was bombed.
Which was not an unusual story in those days.
And that my family had sent me to live with her on the farm.
I would get up-- you know, they would get up early.
There were no clocks that I remember.
And the-- the animals would be taken care of, and then they would be let out of the-- out of the barn.
And I, my job was to watch that they didn't go into the-- in the fields.
They raised potatoes and peas.
There were cherry trees and apple trees.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] they mainly-- I don't remember them going to market to sell any of this.
It was mostly for self.
The food that they ate through the year, they grew for themselves.
They had this big hearth oven.
Behind it was a bench, which became my place to sleep.
Was-- I don't know-- underneath, they kept the potatoes in the winter so they wouldn't freeze, so they would have them to plant in the spring.
It was-- it was an earth floor, there was no wooden floor.
I believe the light was kerosene.
Their mainstay meat was pork.
They butchered a hog once a year.
She made her own cheese.
She made her own buttermilk, her own sauerkraut.
And I'd trot along.
And, you know, she'd put the-- the milk would become what they call [NON-ENGLISH], and she'd put it into like a cheese cloth and put it on a bench with a stone on top.
And this would become like we know farmer cheese.
Everything was utilized.
Nothing was-- was thrown away.
The buttermilk was used from the butter.
The-- the-- she would--when she milked the cow, she would separate this little bit of milk for this.
The cream would rise to the top, this little bit of milk.
Of course, there were only two cows.
So, you know, I'm not talking about a lot, I'm talking little.
There were no children for me to play with, at all.
She had a-- she had one daughter, and the daughter was married and had a baby.
They came once to visit.
They stayed, I don't know, a couple weeks.
She had an uncle, an elderly man who came once to visit.
Otherwise, it was a very lonely existence.
The-- there were always the German patrols through the area.
And from time to time, the partisans came.
There was one man in particular, and a woman-- a young woman.
And they would come and they would look at me.
They would never talk to me, but they would make sure that I was there.
Because when my uncle placed me there, he told them if he ever came or not, that the partisans were aware of where I was.
And they would periodically check on me.
Not that he could have done anything about it, but it was kind of like a checkmate not to throw me out.
I don't know, she was a kind woman.
So I don't know whether she would have or not.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes, I did.
I-- I-- my uncle was very active in the area.
He-- the Germans would come and confiscate food, and he blew up the storage places.
He blew up trains.
And yes, I was aware of this.
They were constantly-- where they could-- they were harassing.
And we would hear.
And the two weeks I spent with them-- and we were constantly on the move-- there was sporadic fighting.
So yes, I was aware of what was going on.
Mostly, we moved from place to place.
It was-- it was rainy part of the year, and was very muddy and cold.
And it may have been the beginning of the end for the Germans, because they were really, at that point, moving around so much.
And the partisans had been harassing them so badly that it was almost like a constant movement.
There was no-- we'd stop to eat or rest, but we weren't in one place for very long.
